addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

input.command: Get-Service | Where-Object { $_.StartType -eq 'Automatic' -and $_.Status -eq 'Running' } | Select-Object Name, DisplayName, Status | Sort-Object Name | Format-Table -AutoSize
input.description: List all running auto-start services

Let me also examine the `configInfo` directory under Server to see what configuration details are stored there.

content.thinking:
The NvrChlRecordDaysInfo.xml shows:
1. There are 4 NVR devices connected (GUIDs: 699AF410, 699AF455, 699AF513, 699AF572)
2. They each have disk storage: sata disks of 3.8TB, 7.6TB
3. The recordings are stored on the NVR devices themselves (they have their own disks), not on the local PC's C drive

The GUIDs I've seen appear to be NVRs (Network Video Recorders):
- NVR 1 and 2 each have a single 3.8TB SATA disk
- NVR 4 has two disks (3.8TB and 7.6TB)
- The zero free space values might just indicate the NVR storage metrics aren't being reported locally

The actual video recordings are stored directly on the NVR devices themselves, not on the local PC. The VMS server software running on the PC manages and monitors these NVRs, but the video data lives on their internal hard drives. Since the user is asking about a "storagedata" folder that doesn't exist on C:\, I need to figure out if they're looking for where the VMS stores its local configuration data (which would be in `C:\ProgramData\nvms\`) or if "storagedata" is a specific folder name they've encountered elsewhere. I should check the registry and VMS database for any references to "storagedata" to see if it's configured as a storage path somewhere.

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture:

`C:\StorageData` contains:
1. `00000054.dat` - 8GB file (8589934592 bytes = 8GB) - this is video recording data
2. `RecordInfoDB` - 1GB file (1073741824 bytes = 1GB) - this is the recording index/database

These are the local video recording storage files for Ossia VMS Standard. The `StorageServer.exe` service manages this.

`C:\IntelligentAnalysis` contains SQLite databases for:
- attendance records
- vehicle detection
- face recognition albums
- task management and other analytics

Now I need to verify that `StorageServer.exe` is actually the service writing to `C:\StorageData`, and check what's in the `C:\capture` and `C:\backup` directories as well.

Looking at the services list, I notice `StorageServerApp` isn't showing as running, even though `StorageServer.exe` exists in the Server folder with recent log entries from today. Let me check the `ServerTrayAllServer.xml` configuration to see if it's disabled or if it runs under a different service name, possibly as part of `ApplicationServerApp`.

| `00000054.dat` | 8 GB | קובץ גולמי של הקלטות וידאו (ערוץ 0x54 = 84 בדצימלי) |

זהו מסד אחסון מקומי של מערכת המצלמות **Ossia VMS Standard** — הוידאו מצלמות שמוגדרות להקלטה מקומית בשרת (בניגוד לאלה שמקליטות ב-NVR עצמו).

| `IntelligentAnalysisServerApp` | `IntelligentAnalysisServer.exe` | AI: זיהוי פנים, כלי-רכב, נוכחות → כותב ל-`C:\IntelligentAnalysis\` |
| `MediaTransferServerApp` | `MediaTransferServer.exe` | העברת סטרימינג |

| `TVWallServerApp` | `TVWallServer.exe` | ניהול מסכי TV Wall |
| **`StorageServerApp`** | `StorageServer.exe` | **→ כותב ל-`C:\StorageData\`** |

#### שירותים אוטומטיים נוספים
| שירות | תפקיד |
|-------|--------|

- `OneDrive Startup/Reporting`

- `Office Automatic Updates`

### תיקיות C:\ אוטומטיות נוספות שנמצאו

| תיקייה | מה כותב לשם | תוכן |
|--------|------------|------|
| `C:\StorageData` | `StorageServerApp` | הקלטות וידאו מקומיות |
| `C:\IntelligentAnalysis` | `IntelligentAnalysisServerApp` | מסדי DB של זיהוי AI |
| `C:\dbbackup` | VMS (backup scheduler) | גיבוי יומי של config ב-00:00 |
| `C:\capture` | לקוח VMS | צילומי מסך מהמצלמות |
| `C:\dump` | VMS Server | קבצי crash dump |
| `C:\jbclock` | JB Superlog (`C:\Program Files (x86)\JB Superlog`) | מערכת נוכחות/שעון נוכחות עם גיבויים חודשיים |

content.text: תרשום את ההוראות בעיברית

כדי לעצור את שירות האחסון המקומי של Ossia VMS:

**שיטה 1 — דרך שירותי Windows:**
1. לחץ `Win + R` ← הקלד `services.msc` ← אנטר
2. חפש `StorageServerApp`
3. לחץ ימני ← **עצור**
4. לחץ ימני שוב ← **מאפיינים** ← שנה סוג הפעלה ל-**מושבת**

**שיטה 2 — דרך PowerShell כמנהל:**
1. לחץ ימני על כפתור Start ← **Windows PowerShell (Admin)**
2. הרץ:
```powershell
Stop-Service -Name StorageServerApp -Force
Set-Service -Name StorageServerApp -StartupType Disabled
```

**לביטול קבוע של האחסון המקומי ב-VMS:**
1. פתח `C:\Program Files\Ossia VMS Standard\Client\MonitorClient.exe`
2. התחבר כ-admin
3. עבור להגדרות → **אחסון** → **ניהול דיסקים**
4. הסר את `C:\StorageData` מרשימת הדיסקים

The hook is still failing. The issue might be that the permission I added is for a specific file path, but the hook might be trying to write to a different path, or the permission syntax isn't matching correctly.

Let me look at this differently - the hook seems to be an "agent" type hook (it says "Agent hook condition was not met"). Looking at the Stop hooks in settings.json, I don't see any agent-type hook. 

But wait - the error says "Agent hook condition was not met" - this is the format for agent-type hooks. Maybe this is a hook defined somewhere else, or it's in a project-level settings file.

Actually, looking more carefully - the user has a Stop hook in their global settings.json that I can see. 

There could be a project-level settings file in the `.claude` directory that contains an agent-type Stop hook, or the agent hook might be attempting to use the Write tool during its execution, and even though the session has Write permissions for the file, the agent's own permissions might be more restrictive. The permission pattern might not be matching correctly if the hook is running from a different working directory or using a relative path. I should try a broader Write permission for the `.claude` directory like `"Write(C:\\Users\\yaniv\\.claude\\*)"` instead of the specific file path, and also check if there's a project-level settings file that contains the session-log hook. Agent hooks are different from command hooks, so this one must be coming from somewhere else—possibly a project-level settings file at `.claude/.claude/settings.json`. I need to track down where this agent hook is defined and either fix the permissions or remove it if it's not needed.
